summary: Uncle Bob argues that after five decades of programming in dozens of languages, Clojure (a modern Lisp) has emerged as his favorite and the language he believes will eventually become standard. His singular reason is 'economy of expression' - Clojure's near-absence of syntax and grammar allows programmers to express problems clearly with minimal effort. He demonstrates this by showing how the same program requires far less code in Clojure than Java, while addressing common objections about parentheses, performance, dynamic typing, and tooling. He concludes that Lisp's minimal syntax, functional nature, and remarkable persistence over 60+ years make it uniquely suited for building large systems.
key_points: Clojure's power comes from one thing: economy of expression enabled by almost no syntax or grammar
Despite initial skepticism, minimal syntax proved far more conducive to building large systems than heavyweight languages like C++ or Java
Dynamic typing concerns can be addressed through tests and clojure/spec, and the syntax savings are worth the tradeoff
Lisp has survived for 60+ years despite repeated attempts to kill it, outlasting languages like Smalltalk and Forth
New languages offer nothing revolutionary - they're just recombinations of old ideas with extra syntax baggage
money_quotes: The language has almost no syntax or grammar.

I've come to a conclusion. My favorite language of all, the language that I think will outlast all the others, the language that I believe will eventually become the standard language that all programmers use… is Lisp.
What I found, instead, was that the minimal syntax of Clojure is far more conducive to building large systems, than the heavier syntax of Java or C++.
OK, I get it. You like static typing. Fine. You use a nice statically typed language, and I'll use Clojure. And I'll be in Scotland before ye.

The thing about those new languages is that there's nothing new in any of them. They're just made up of the pieces of old languages that they cut up and shook in a jar and then dumped out in a game of language Yahtzee.
It's the language that refuses to die. We've tried to kill it many times. But like the annoying neighborhood stray cat it keeps … coming … back.
Finally, Lisp is functional. And the future is looking very functional to me.
key_insight: A language's power lies not in its features but in its economy of expression - the less syntax standing between thought and code, the better equipped you are to build large, maintainable systems.
